evals.name: orders-rest-controller
evals.prompt: Implementing the orders REST API in our Spring Boot Kotlin service. Endpoints: POST /orders (create), GET /orders/{id}, GET /orders?status=...&page=&size=. Need request validation (Jakarta), proper error responses (problem+json), and the controller-service-repo split we follow. Show controller, DTOs, validation annotations, and how errors translate to HTTP responses. Don't show the full service body, just the layering.
evals.expected_output: @RestController with @Valid request bodies, DTOs with jakarta.validation annotations, ResponseEntity or @ResponseStatus exceptions, problem+json error mapping via @ControllerAdvice or ProblemDetail, separation of controller/service/repository.
assertions: @RestController class with all three endpoints (POST /orders, GET /orders/{id}, GET /orders with query params)
Request DTOs use jakarta.validation annotations (@NotBlank, @Positive, @Valid etc.)
Controller methods use @Valid on request bodies
Error handling produces problem+json (ProblemDetail or @ControllerAdvice with ResponseEntity<ProblemDetail>)
Pagination params (page/size) handled (Pageable or explicit params)
Layer split visible: controller delegates to service interface, not data layer directly

evals.name: user-service-transactional
evals.prompt: Service-layer code for user management in Kotlin Spring Boot. Operations: createUser (insert + send welcome email), changeEmail (update + audit log + invalidate sessions), deactivateUser (soft delete + cascade flag inventory). Each needs proper transactional boundaries — what runs inside the tx vs after, propagation, rollback rules. Show the @Service class with each method annotated correctly and explain the boundary choices.
evals.expected_output: @Service class, @Transactional with appropriate propagation (REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW where relevant), side effects (email, audit) explicitly placed inside or outside tx with reasoning, rollback rules for checked exceptions if needed.
assertions: @Service class with three methods (createUser, changeEmail, deactivateUser)
At least one method uses propagation other than default (REQUIRES_NEW or NOT_SUPPORTED) where it makes sense
Side effects (email send, audit log) discussed in tx context — either inside tx via TransactionalEventListener / outside tx explicitly
Rollback rules addressed (rollbackFor or noRollbackFor where Kotlin checked exceptions concerns arise) or explicit reasoning given
Reasoning behind boundary choice explained per method
